text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

The loneliest job in sport and the athletes who have to face the highs and lows of the Games without any teammates

Every athlete dreams of receiving the call to carry their nation’s flag at the opening ceremony of theOlympic Games. Few would envy those who must choose which of their nation’s decorated stars will get to perform the role. For some countries though, there isn’t a choice to make.

In 2016, Etimoni Timuani made headlines after he made up the entirety of Tuvalu’s delegation in Rio. The sight of the sprinter’s solo journey through the opening ceremony made headlines far beyond the borders of his small island nation.

The image of an athlete carrying their country’s flag is one viewers are used to, but he was the only one who also had to carry the hopes of his country alone. He was widely dubbed the “loneliest athlete in Rio”.

Eight years later, Tuvalu is sending double the number of competitors to Paris – and this time around, there are four other nations who only have one representative.

At first or even second glance, Belize, Nauru, Somalia, and Liechtenstein have little in common. They span four continents, are vastly different in size and all speak different languages. Even the most proficient Only Connect player would struggle to find the link between them.

They are united by the fact that their respective hopes at the 2024 Summer Olympics rest on the shoulders of just four individuals. The countries may have a combined population of nearly 20 million people, but they are collectively sending fewer athletes than Iceland.

Liechtenstein’s hopes rest on the performances of cross-country cyclist Romano Puntener while Somalia will pin their hopes on 800m runner Ali Idow Hassan.

Remarkably, given that more than 10,000 athletes will compete in Paris, Belize and Nauru’s athletes could well end up competing directly against one another. Both Belize’s Shaun Gill and Nauru’s Winzar Kakiouea are due to battle for the glittering jewel in athletics’ crown, the 100m.

Of these four athletes, Puntener and Kakiouea are making their Olympic debuts while the others are appearing at their second Games, having competed atTokyo 2020. None of them have ever made the journey alone.

Each athlete’s story is different, even if they are united by their statuses as their countries’ lone offerings. Gill recently graduated with a degree in engineering from Texas A&M University. He may not have any fellow Belizean athletes to socialise with, but he won’t be short of familiar faces – more than twenty current or former Texas A&M students will be competing in Paris.

Similarly, Hassan has based his training camps in Kenya and Ethiopia and will not be well known to many of his fellow athletes.

Hassan is also the most likely of the four to be in medal contention, having recorded a personal best earlier this year and finished seventh out of a tough field in the final of the African Athletics Championship last month.

Kakiouea’s journey to the Games is perhaps the most remarkable of them all, given the country he will be representing in the 100m does not have a single racetrack. The tiny Micronesian island is five times smaller than the city in which he will represent it.

Its entire population could fit inside the Stade de France to watch him race six times over. Kakiouea tellsihis goal is to break the Nauruan 100m national record, which he jointly holds, at these Games. When he steps out on the track, all 12,883 of his countrymen will be willing him to do so from half a world away.

Athletes that compete in solo events have long been regarded as different to those that compete in team sports. The pressure they experience is theirs to bear alone. The warmth of their triumphs is theirs to bask in. The weight of their disappointments cannot be passed on. These athletes do not have the security that being part of a team provides.

Pundits and commentators often peddle dichotomies like this to build up the competitors even further in the eyes of the audience. It makes the victories of those athletes all the more glorious.

We tell ourselves that Jessica Ennis-Hill,Mo Farah, andGreg Rutherfordwon those 2012 gold medals entirely alone, as though they weren’t part of aTeam GBdelegation that included 541 athletes and weren’t competing in front of 80,000 fans willing them to victory.

That is not to disparage those athletes or their accomplishments but merely to emphasise the point that the benefits of being part of a team far extend the limits of a competitor’s event of choice.

As of yet, none of the four nations in question have announced who their flagbearers will be, but this is likely because the answer is a formality.

It is also not known whether the athletes themselves are aware of the fact that there are three other competitors in theOlympic Villagewho can genuinely claim to know how they are feeling.

In many ways, these athletes will be privy to a feeling far rarer than that of a medal winner. By the time of the closing ceremony in August, around one thousand medals will have been given out across more than two hundred events. The crown of the Games’ loneliest athlete is a far more exclusive title.

Eight years on from Timuani’s viral flag-bearing appearance, that title will be split four ways. The Tuvaluan sprinter tied for last position in his chosen event in 2016. He will be pleased to see that, this year, the title that will be shared will be the one he made famous.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: The four nations with just one athlete at Paris Olympics 2024
author: Joe Santamaria
description: The loneliest job in sport and the athletes who have to face the highs and lows of the Games without any teammates
